title: Repair or replace: a decision framework
subtitle: Embodied carbon, repair cost, and where to find parts
excerpt: A new appliance carries embodied carbon from manufacturing, often equivalent to months or years of running it. Repairing and extending its life delays the manufacturing impact and keeps material out of landfill. The decision framework here uses repair cost as a fraction of replacement cost, weighted by remaining expected life.
sourceNotes: WRAP Electrical Product Material Flows report. Right to Repair EU Directive 2024/1799. Restart Project repairability data. Carbon Trust embodied carbon in household appliances.

content.text: Making a new washing machine produces around 200 to 300 kg of CO₂e in embodied carbon. The UK grid runs at roughly 200 g CO₂e per kWh. That is the same as running the machine for 6 to 9 weeks before the operating carbon from the new one overtakes the old one. Keeping an existing appliance going for five more years saves that manufacturing carbon entirely.

A common rule of thumb: if the repair cost is more than 50% of the replacement cost, replacing is usually the better choice. Adjust for the remaining life. A 10-year-old appliance at the end of its usual lifespan should clear a lower bar than a 2-year-old still in peak life. The rule breaks down when a new appliance is much more energy efficient than the old one. It also breaks down when parts are no longer available.
Manufacturer's own spare parts: most major appliance brands sell parts via their website. Look up the model number (usually on a label inside the door or on the back).
Third-party parts: eSpares (UK), Ransom Spares, and 4ourhouse stock parts for most UK appliance brands.
Repair cafes and community repair events. The Restart Project (therestartproject.org) runs a directory of free repair events in the UK.
iFixit: free repair guides for electronics, appliances, and devices.
Right to Repair (EU Directive, UK policy)

EU Directive 2024/1799 gives EU consumers a right to request repair from manufacturers for common goods. The list covers washing machines, dishwashers, televisions, bicycles, clothing, and smartphones. Repairs must be at a reasonable cost. Parts must be available for a set number of years after manufacture. The UK government is consulting on its own version. Check gov.uk for the current status.
When replacing is the right call
Safety. An appliance with electrical faults, gas leaks, or structural failures that cannot be fixed should be replaced.
No parts available: without parts, repair is not possible regardless of cost.
Efficiency. A new A-rated appliance using half the electricity of an old E-rated one may be worth replacing. The energy saved over 5 years should cover the embodied carbon of the new unit.
